docs.title: Configuration reference (cookwhat.config.json)
docs.text:
Everything that makes a plan "yours" lives here. Run cookwhat init to create the file with sensible defaults, then edit it. Claude reads this on every plan and treats it as the rulebook; the plan check validator enforces the hard rules.

Edit the file directly, or use the CLI:

cookwhat config show
cookwhat config get proteinRules.redMeatMaxPerWeek
cookwhat config set proteinRules.redMeatMaxPerWeek 2
cookwhat config set ingredients.dislikes '["liver","cilantro"]'

docs.title: household
docs.text: Field Meaning people, adults, kids Who you're cooking for (context for Claude). defaultServings Servings used when a meal doesn't specify one, and the base for shopping-list scaling. notes Free text ("toddler, mild spice"; "we love leftovers for lunch").

docs.title: schedule
docs.text: Field Meaning daysToPlan Which days to plan, e.g. ["Mon".."Sun"]. Trim if you don't cook nightly. mealsPerDay Slots per day. Default ["dinner"]; add "lunch"/"breakfast". weekendDays Days that get the weekend time budget (more involved cooking).

docs.title: preferences
docs.text: Field Meaning cuisines.love / .ok / .avoid Prioritize, allow, or exclude cuisines. avoid is a hard rule. preferredSites Reputable sites Claude pulls recipes from, in priority order. avoidSites Sites to never use (hard rule).

docs.title: proteinRules — weekly balance (enforced by plan check)
docs.text:
Field Meaning redMeatMaxPerWeek e.g. 1 for "red meat once a week". Exceeding it is an error. poultryMaxPerWeek Soft cap (warning). seafoodMinPerWeek Aim for at least this much fish/seafood (warning if under). vegetarianMinPerWeek Minimum meatless dinners (warning if under). noRepeatProteinTwoDaysInRow Warn when the same protein lands on back-to-back days. classification Maps protein words → categories (red_meat, poultry, seafood, vegetarian). Add words to fix mis-categorization.

Pork is treated as red meat by default — move it to poultry/its own bucket via classification if you prefer.

docs.title: ingredients
docs.text: Field Meaning dislikes Hard avoid — a match anywhere in a meal is an error (e.g. "liver"). allergies Hard, safety-critical avoid (errors). favorites Ingredients to lean into (guidance for Claude). pantryStaples Things you always have; excluded from shopping lists. Single words match as whole words ("salt" also hides "kosher salt"); keep meal-specific items like "jasmine rice" off the list so they still get listed.

docs.title: constraints
docs.text: Field Meaning maxActiveMinutesWeeknight Hands-on time budget Mon–Fri (warning if exceeded). maxActiveMinutesWeekend Budget for weekend days. weeknightDifficulty Free-text guidance ("easy-to-medium"). leftoversOk Whether Claude can plan dishes that intentionally make leftovers. avoidRepeatWithinDays Don't repeat a meal cooked within this many days (unless it's a deliberate redo). useSeasonalProduce Bias toward in-season ingredients.

docs.title: nutrition
docs.text: Field Meaning goals Tags Claude leans toward ("vegetable-forward", "whole-grains-over-refined", …). notes Free text ("lower sodium", "high protein for training").

docs.title: shopping
docs.text: Field Meaning excludePantryStaples Drop pantryStaples from generated lists. aisleOrder Category order for the shopping list, matching your store's layout. These are also the valid category values for ingredients.

docs.title: Example tweaks
docs.text:
# Family of 4, two veg nights minimum, red meat twice a week
cookwhat config set household.defaultServings 4
cookwhat config set proteinRules.vegetarianMinPerWeek 2
cookwhat config set proteinRules.redMeatMaxPerWeek 2

# Only cook Sun–Thu
cookwhat config set schedule.daysToPlan '["Sun","Mon","Tue","Wed","Thu"]'

# Never any cilantro or liver
cookwhat config set ingredients.dislikes '["liver","cilantro","raw oyster"]'

docs.title: How to use cookwhat
docs.text:
cookwhat plans balanced weekly dinners from reputable recipe sites, keeps a rating history so you can re-cook the winners, and turns any set menu into a consolidated shopping list.

The trick: Claude is the chef's brain, the CLI is the kitchen notebook. You talk to Claude (in the Claude app or VS Code with this repo open); Claude searches the web for real recipes, designs a balanced week around your rules, and saves everything through the cookwhat command. You can also run the CLI directly any time.

docs.title: 1. One-time setup
docs.text:
node bin/cookwhat.js init      # creates cookwhat.config.json + data/ folders

Optionally link it so you can type cookwhat instead of node bin/cookwhat.js:

npm link        # then: cookwhat help

Now edit cookwhat.config.json to make it yours — household size, "red meat once a week", "I hate liver", favorite cuisines and sites, weeknight time budget, etc. Every option is explained in CONFIG.md. You only do this once (and tweak it whenever your tastes change).

docs.title: 2. Build a menu (the AI part)
docs.text:
Open this repo in the Claude app or VS Code and just ask. Claude reads your config and CLAUDE.md, then runs the loop for you.

"Build me a balanced dinner menu for next week."

Claude will: 1. Read your preferences and your redos/recent history. 2. Search the web (Serious Eats, NYT Cooking, Bon Appétit, …) for real, linked recipes. 3. Draft a balanced week (varied proteins/cuisines, weeknight-friendly, respecting your protein rules and dislikes). 4. Save it and run cookwhat plan check to confirm it follows your rules. 5. Show you the plan with links so you can swap anything.

Helpful follow-ups: - "Swap Thursday for something vegetarian and faster." - "Include my top redo this week." - "Make Saturday a project recipe; keep weeknights under 30 minutes." - "Looks good — set it."

Prefer to do it by hand? See the CLI reference — you can plan new, plan add, plan check, and plan set yourself.

docs.title: 3. Generate the shopping list
docs.text:
Once a menu is set:

"Make my shopping list for this week." → or → cookwhat shopping <date>

You get a checklist grouped by store aisle, with quantities consolidated across recipes (e.g. garlic from three dishes becomes one line) and pantry staples removed. Need more servings for guests? cookwhat shopping <date> --servings 6. It's saved to data/shopping/<week>.md.

docs.title: 4. Rate meals → smarter "redos"
docs.text:
After you cook, tell Claude or log it directly:

"We loved the Thai basil chicken — 5 stars, definitely a redo."

cookwhat rate --title "Thai Basil Chicken" --rating 5 --redo \
  --cuisine thai --protein chicken --url "https://www.seriouseats.com/..."

Then cookwhat redos surfaces your best-loved meals to seed future weeks, and cookwhat history is your searchable cooking log.

docs.title: 5. CLI reference
docs.text:
Run node bin/cookwhat.js help for the full list. Highlights:

Command What it does init Create config + data folders config show / config set <path> <value> View / edit preferences plan new [--week DATE] Start a draft menu (DATE = any day that week) plan add --week D --json '{...}' Add one meal plan import --week D --file plan.json Import a whole week plan show [DATE] Show a menu + rule check plan check [DATE] Validate against your config plan set [DATE] Finalize (blocked if rules fail) plan list List all menus rate --title .. --rating 1-5 [--redo] Log a cooked meal history [--cuisine x] [--min-rating 4] Browse the cooking log redos [--top N] Best meals to cook again shopping [DATE] [--servings N] Consolidated shopping list

Dates: a "week" is identified by its Monday; pass any date in the week and cookwhat snaps to that Monday.

docs.title: 6. Keeping your data
docs.text:
Your menus, history, and lists live in data/ and are committed to git, so your cooking history survives across machines and sessions. After setting a menu or adding ratings, commit:

git add data/ && git commit -m "Menu + ratings for week of <date>"

Claude will offer to do this for you.

docs.title: How it fits together
docs.text:
you ──▶ Claude (web search + judgment)
                         │  reads cookwhat.config.json (your rules)
                         │  reads redos / history (your tastes)
                         ▼
                   cookwhat CLI  ──▶  data/menus/<week>.json   (the plan)
                         │                data/history.json     (ratings)
                         ▼
                   plan check (rules)  ──▶  plan set
                         ▼
                   shopping  ──▶  data/shopping/<week>.md   (your grocery run)

docs.title: Mon — Salmon Bowl
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: japanese Protein: salmon Tags: weeknight, quick, dairy-free, bowl

Complete bowl: seared salmon + rice + fresh veg all in one recipe.

docs.title: Tue — One-Pan Italian Chicken and Rice
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: italian Protein: chicken Tags: weeknight, one-pan, dairy-free

Dairy-free, whole meal: chicken + rice + tomatoes in one pan.

docs.title: Wed — Shrimp Fried Rice with Sautéed Garlic Spinach
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: chinese Protein: shrimp Tags: weeknight, quick, dairy-free, one-pan

Cook fried rice first, then sauté spinach with garlic in the same pan — wilts in 2 min. Cook rice Tuesday night so it's day-old.

docs.title: Thu — Easy Korean Beef Bowls
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: korean Protein: beef Tags: weeknight, quick

Your one red-meat meal this week.

docs.title: Fri — Chicken Souvlaki
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: mediterranean Protein: chicken Tags: weeknight, grill, summer

Marinate while you prep; tzatziki = yogurt + grated cucumber + garlic.

docs.title: Week of June 22, 2026
docs.text: Day Meal Cuisine Protein Source Mon Chicken au Poivre with Fondant Potatoes and Haricots Verts french chicken The Kitchn Tue Shrimp Pad Thai thai shrimp The Woks of Life Wed One-Pot Chicken Teriyaki with Vegetables and Rice japanese chicken The Kitchn Thu Garlicky Shrimp Linguine mediterranean shrimp The Mediterranean Dish Fri Mediterranean Baked Fish with Tomatoes, Capers and Couscous mediterranean fish The Mediterranean Dish Sat Roasted Pork Loin with Potatoes and Carrots american pork The Kitchn

docs.title: Mon — Chicken au Poivre with Fondant Potatoes and Haricots Verts
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: french Protein: chicken Tags: weeknight, dinner-party, dairy-low

Fondant potatoes: cut into thick cylinders, brown both sides in oil, then braise in chicken broth with thyme and garlic until meltingly tender. Start potatoes first — they take ~30 min. Blanch beans last 5 min.

docs.title: Tue — Shrimp Pad Thai
docs.text:
Source: The Woks of Life Cuisine: thai Protein: shrimp Tags: weeknight, dairy-free

Soak noodles in cold water while you prep everything else. Complete meal — no sides needed.

docs.title: Wed — One-Pot Chicken Teriyaki with Vegetables and Rice
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: japanese Protein: chicken Tags: weeknight, dairy-free, one-pot

Complete one-pot. Add bok choy in the last few minutes so it stays crisp.

docs.title: Thu — Garlicky Shrimp Linguine
docs.text:
Source: The Mediterranean Dish Cuisine: mediterranean Protein: shrimp Tags: weeknight, dairy-free

Arugula wilts into the hot pasta — adds a peppery green without a separate side.

docs.title: Fri — Mediterranean Baked Fish with Tomatoes, Capers and Couscous
docs.text:
Source: The Mediterranean Dish Cuisine: mediterranean Protein: fish Tags: weeknight, dairy-free

Couscous just needs boiling water — start it 5 min before the fish comes out. Spoon the tomato-caper sauce over both.

docs.title: Sat — Roasted Pork Loin with Potatoes and Carrots
docs.text:
Source: The Kitchn Cuisine: american Protein: pork Tags: weekend, project, dairy-free

20 min active, 55 min in the oven. Roast veg underneath to catch all the drippings.
